title: Best Decentralised Social Media Platforms 2026: DeHub vs Bluesky, Farcaster, Lens & Mastodon
html:
Looking for the best decentralized social media platforms in 2026? The strongest options right now are Bluesky (the biggest, most mainstream network), Mastodon and the wider fediverse (open, non-profit, no ads), Farcaster and Lens (protocol-first and developer-led), and DeHub, the decentralised social network built specifically to pay creators through watch-to-earn, on-chain tradable subscriptions and up to 90% ad-revenue share. Which one is right for you depends on a single question that most comparison articles skip: do you just want to leave Big Tech, or do you want to get paid while you do it?

That is the axis we compare on below. Plenty of decentralised networks free you from a central owner and an opaque algorithm. Very few of them turn your audience into income. This guide covers each major platform fairly — including where DeHub is smaller and newer — so you can pick the one that fits your goals rather than the one with the loudest marketing. If you want the underlying concepts first, start with decentralised social media explained for UK users.

What "decentralised social media" actually means in 2026

Before the rankings, a quick definition, because the label gets stretched. A genuinely decentralised social network removes the single point of control that defines platforms like Instagram, X and TikTok. In practice that shows up in three ways:

  • Ownership of identity and audience. Your account, your followers and your content are not held hostage by one company that can delete or shadow-ban them at will.
  • Open protocols or open source. The rules of the network are inspectable and, ideally, forkable — anyone can build a client or run a server.
  • Transparent distribution. Reach is determined by a published, chronological or community-driven system rather than a black-box engagement algorithm.

Some platforms nail all three. Others are decentralised in architecture but still behave like a walled garden in practice. And a separate, smaller group adds a fourth pillar — native creator monetisation — where the network is designed so that attention flows value back to the people who make the content. That fourth pillar is where the field thins out dramatically, and it is where DeHub, the decentralised social network, concentrates.

One housekeeping note before we start: the DeHub covered here is the Web3 social platform at dehub.io. It is unrelated to DePaul University's "DeHUB" student portal or Rowan University's "DEHub" digital engineering hub — same-sounding names, completely different things.

The best decentralized social media platforms 2026, compared

Here is the honest state of play, platform by platform. User figures are approximate ranges drawn from mid-2026 third-party estimates; every network below reports metrics differently, so treat them as orders of magnitude, not precise counts.

Bluesky — the mainstream leader

Bluesky is the closest thing the space has to a household name and, for most people, the easiest decentralized twitter alternative to adopt. Built on the open AT Protocol, it had grown to tens of millions of registered accounts by 2026 — well north of forty million — with monthly active users estimated in the low-to-mid tens of millions. It feels familiar because it is deliberately Twitter-shaped: short posts, follows, reposts, quote posts, and a chronological "Following" feed alongside custom feeds you can choose or build.

The genuinely good parts: account portability is real, you can host your own data, custom feeds put algorithmic control in users' hands, and moderation is composable rather than dictated from one head office. If your only goal is to escape a single owner and keep a large, active audience, Bluesky is the safest bet in 2026.

The honest limitation: there is no native token and no built-in way for creators to earn. Bluesky has floated subscription features for extras, but at its core it does not pay you for the attention you generate. It is a better public square, not a creator business. Ad revenue, tips and paid content are not part of the base experience the way they are on a social media that pays creators.

Best for: writers, journalists, communities and anyone who wants a big, mainstream, decentralised feed without thinking about crypto.

Mastodon and the fediverse — open, non-profit, no monetisation

Mastodon is the elder statesman of decentralised social. It is open-source software run as a non-profit, structured as thousands of independent servers ("instances") that interoperate over the ActivityPub protocol — the fediverse. By 2026 Mastodon itself sits at roughly three-quarters of a million to one million monthly active users, part of a broader fediverse (Pixelfed, PeerTube and others) that has surpassed eleven million total accounts with perhaps one to two million active across all of it.

What Mastodon does brilliantly is community governance and the absence of commercial incentives. There is no algorithm optimising for outrage, no advertising, and no company that can be acquired and enshittified. For many users that is precisely the point.

The trade-off is the mirror image of that strength: there is no monetisation at all, by design. Mastodon is not trying to pay creators, and the non-profit ethos means it likely never will natively. Onboarding also asks a bit more of newcomers — you have to pick a server before you can start — though that friction has eased over the years.

Best for: communities, hobbyists and privacy-minded users who want a calm, ad-free, non-commercial home and do not care about earning.

Farcaster — developer-first, sufficiently decentralised

Farcaster is a decentralised social protocol that gained real mindshare among the crypto and builder crowd, largely thanks to "Frames" — interactive mini-apps embedded directly in posts. It is well-engineered and its most popular client, Warpcast, offers a polished experience. Farcaster leans on the concept of "sufficient decentralisation": identity and social graph live on-chain, while the bulk of content lives on off-chain hubs.

The reality check on scale: despite significant funding, Farcaster's daily active users have hovered around the tens of thousands in 2026 — roughly sixty thousand at recent counts, down from earlier peaks. It is a vibrant, high-signal niche, not a mass-market network. The culture and vocabulary are also unapologetically crypto-native, which is a feature for insiders and a barrier for everyone else.

Best for: Web3 builders, crypto-natives and people who want to experiment with on-chain social apps and Frames.

Lens — a social graph as infrastructure

Lens (now running as Lens Protocol on its own chain) takes the most infrastructural approach: it treats your profile, follows and posts as on-chain assets — often as NFTs — that any application can plug into. The pitch is that you own your social graph as portable data and carry it between any Lens-powered app.

Lens is arguably the purest expression of "you own your social graph", and it is a serious piece of developer infrastructure. But adoption remains modest: total accounts number in the hundreds of thousands (around 650,000), with daily active users generally estimated in the low tens of thousands across all clients. Taken together, neither Lens nor Farcaster had cracked sustained six-figure daily usage by 2026, despite more than a combined US$240 million in funding between them — a useful reminder that decentralised architecture alone does not summon an audience.

Best for: developers building social apps, and users who prioritise owning a portable, composable social graph above reach.

The rest — Minds, Hive, Nostr

Three more deserve a mention:

  • Nostr is not a platform but a radically simple, censorship-resistant protocol built around cryptographic keys and relays. It is beloved by Bitcoiners, supports "zaps" (Bitcoin Lightning tips), and is genuinely hard to censor — but it is technical, fragmented across clients, and small.
  • Minds is an open-source network that has offered crypto-based rewards and boosts for years. It is a legitimate option for creators wanting some token upside, though its reach is limited.
  • Hive is a blockchain-based platform where posts can earn the native token through community voting. It has a dedicated base and real on-chain rewards, but its rewards model is idiosyncratic and its audience is niche.

All three prove that "decentralised" and "pays something" can coexist. None of them combines mainstream-grade media features — video, live streaming, subscriptions — with creator payouts at any scale.

DeHub — decentralised social that actually pays creators

This is the intersection almost no one else occupies: a full-featured, media-first social network that is decentralised and built from the ground up to pay the people who make the content. That is the entire design premise of DeHub, the decentralised social network.

Here is what that looks like in practice as a crypto social media app:

  • Content is minted on-chain the moment you upload. Ownership is provable and yours from second one — not licensed away in a terms-of-service you never read.
  • A chronological, transparent feed. No opaque algorithm deciding who sees you. Distribution is legible.
  • Tokenised uploads. Publish free, pay-per-view, or token-gated — you choose how each piece of content is accessed and priced.
  • On-chain tradable subscriptions with resale royalties. Subscriptions to a creator are on-chain assets that fans can resell, and the creator keeps earning royalties on the resale. This is one of DeHub's most distinctive mechanics; there is a full breakdown in tokenised subscriptions explained.
  • Watch-to-earn bounties. Viewers can be rewarded for their attention, turning passive scrolling into a two-sided value exchange.
  • Up to 90% ad-revenue share flows back to the ecosystem rather than to a platform's shareholders.
  • Live streaming on Livepeer that has scaled to 50,000+ concurrent viewers, with on-chain live streams and animated tips. If replacing your streaming platform matters, see Web3 live streaming and the decentralised Twitch alternative.
  • Open-source, end-to-end encrypted messaging (XChaCha20-Poly1305, X25519 ECDH), a custom CDN with sub-200ms global latency, DePIN infrastructure, and a play-to-earn arcade including the flagship MMA battle-royale game "Last Chad Standing".

The ecosystem runs on the DHB token ($DHB), primarily on Base (an Ethereum Layer 2), with contracts also on BNB Chain and Polygon. DeHub is built by DeLabs Ltd, a UK company whose co-founders, Mike Hales and Indi Jay Cammish, also founded First Class Agency — the UK's number-one official TikTok LIVE partner agency — so the team understands creator monetisation from the inside.

Now the honest part, because this comparison is only useful if it is fair: DeHub is newer and smaller than Bluesky. If your single priority is the largest possible mainstream audience today, Bluesky wins on raw scale. DeHub's bet is different — that creators will follow the platform that actually pays them and lets them own their audience, not the one with the biggest headcount. If you make content for a living, that is the axis that matters.

Best for: creators and communities who want to own their content, keep their audience, and earn from video, live streams, subscriptions and attention — not just post into a void.

How to choose the best decentralised social media for you

Skip the leaderboard mindset. The "best" platform is the one that matches your actual use case. Here is how to decide.

If you want the biggest mainstream audience

Choose Bluesky. It has the scale, the familiar interface and the lowest barrier to entry. Accept that you are there for reach and conversation, not income.

If you want a calm, non-commercial, ad-free community

Choose Mastodon or another fediverse platform. You get genuine community governance and zero advertising. Accept that there is no monetisation and a slightly steeper onboarding.

If you are a developer or crypto-native experimenter

Choose Farcaster or Lens. You get on-chain identity, composability and a builder culture. Accept that daily active audiences are small and the vocabulary is insider-heavy.

If you want to get paid for your content

Choose DeHub. It is the option purpose-built so that video, live streams, subscriptions and viewer attention generate real, on-chain income for creators, with up to 90% ad-revenue share and content you own outright. Accept that it is younger and smaller than Bluesky — and decide whether ownership and earnings matter more to you than sheer headcount.

If you are specifically leaving TikTok

Read the UK creator's guide to leaving TikTok first. Short-form creators have particular needs — video-first tooling, live streaming and payouts — and the right destination is rarely a text-first network. This is precisely the gap DeHub is built to fill.

The pattern to notice

Line the platforms up and a clear split appears. On one side sit the networks that decentralise ownership and speech — Bluesky, Mastodon, and to a degree Nostr — but leave creator earnings off the table. On the other side sit the networks that decentralise the social graph as infrastructure — Farcaster and Lens — brilliant for builders but small and jargon-heavy. DeHub is deliberately positioned across a third axis: decentralised ownership plus mainstream media features plus native creator payments, in one place.

No single platform is objectively "the best". But if you accept that the most valuable thing a creator has is their audience and their output, the most interesting question in 2026 is not "which network is the biggest?" It is "which network lets me own both of those things and earn from them?" On that question, the field narrows very quickly.

Frequently asked questions

What is the best decentralized social media platform in 2026?

There is no single winner — it depends on your goal. For the biggest mainstream audience, Bluesky leads with tens of millions of users. For a calm, ad-free community, Mastodon and the fediverse are strongest. For developers and crypto-natives, Farcaster and Lens are the most interesting. For creators who want to actually earn from their content through watch-to-earn, tradable subscriptions and ad-revenue share, DeHub is purpose-built for that, though it is newer and smaller than Bluesky.

Which decentralised social network pays creators the most?

Among genuinely decentralised networks, DeHub is designed most explicitly around creator earnings: tokenised pay-per-view and token-gated uploads, on-chain tradable subscriptions with resale royalties, watch-to-earn bounties, on-chain tips on live streams, and up to 90% ad-revenue share to the ecosystem. Bluesky and Mastodon do not pay creators natively; Hive and Minds offer smaller on-chain rewards. Earnings always depend on your audience and engagement, so treat any platform's payout mechanics as opportunity, not a guarantee.

Is Bluesky better than DeHub?

They optimise for different things. Bluesky is bigger and more mainstream, and it is the easier pick if you simply want a large, familiar, decentralised text feed. DeHub is media-first and built to pay creators, with video, live streaming, subscriptions and on-chain ownership — but it is younger and has a smaller audience today. If reach is your priority, Bluesky; if ownership and income are, DeHub.

Are decentralised social media platforms actually safe and legit?

The established ones are legitimate technologies, not scams — Bluesky, Mastodon, Farcaster, Lens and DeHub are all real, working networks, several of them open source and inspectable. As with any platform, do your own diligence: read how monetisation works, understand that token-based rewards carry market risk, and never treat crypto earnings as guaranteed income. Open-source code and on-chain transparency make the credible projects easier to verify than closed Big Tech alternatives.

Do I need cryptocurrency to use a decentralised social network?

Not usually to browse or post. Bluesky and Mastodon involve no crypto at all. On Web3 platforms like DeHub you can explore the feed freely, but earning, tokenised uploads, subscriptions and tips involve the DHB token and a wallet. You can get started without spending anything and add a wallet when you are ready to earn or pay for gated content.

What is the best decentralized Twitter alternative?

For a like-for-like Twitter replacement — short posts, a familiar feed, a large audience — Bluesky is the strongest decentralized twitter alternative in 2026. If you want a Twitter-style experience that also lets you earn from your content and own it on-chain, DeHub is the creator-focused alternative worth trying alongside it.

Try a decentralised network that pays you

The fastest way to understand the difference is to use one. Explore the feed, tokenise an upload, or go live at dehub.io, open the DeHub app in your browser, or download it on Google Play. Own your content, keep your audience, and get paid for the attention you already create.
